stem: A hospital uses a serum ferritin test to screen for iron deficiency anemia. The test has a sensitivity of 99% and a specificity of 86%. The prevalence of iron deficiency anemia in the patient population is estimated to be between 1.9% and 4.2%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has iron deficiency anemia? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(iron deficiency anemia | positive test) ranges from 0.12 to 0.237 as prevalence varies from 1.9% to 4.2%. Point estimate at 3.0% prevalence: 0.179.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.14.
The confidence interval [0.12, 0.237] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (99%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
